stories.title: Starwood Advances 226K-SF Data Center in Fairfax County, Va.
stories.excerpt: Starwood Capital Group is advancing plans for a pair of data centers in Fairfax County, Va., near the Alexandria border. The investment firm filed plans last week for the first building at its Plaza 500 development, according to the Business Journals, which cited Fairfax County records. Plaza 500 South, the proposed two-story data center, would span 226,469 square feet and rise 75 feet. The first phase of construction on the building shell is estimated to cost $5 million. An affiliate of Miami-based Starwood acquired the 34-acre site, adjacent to the Bren Mar neighborhood, for $165 million in 2022. The company is also awaiting approval for a proposed electrical substation that would sit less than 100 feet from nearby homes. The plans have drawn opposition from residents, who earlier this year proposed that Fairfax County swap the Plaza 500 property for a Chantilly site the county plans to sell to Starwood for data center development. Under that proposal, affordable housing would instead be developed near Bren Mar. County officials rejected the idea and are expected to close on the $167 million Chantilly property sale to Starwood next spring. Residents are planning to rally against the Edsall Road substation when the proposal goes before the planning commission in September. Starwood is also developing several other major data center projects in Northern Virginia. The firm is redeveloping the former Washington Commanders training facility in Herndon into two data centers near its Renaissance Tech Park, which is expected to span 2.3 million square feet at full buildout and become Fairfax County’s largest data center complex. Amazon is slated to use the property. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Starwood Capital filed plans for 226K-SF data center in Fairfax County, Va., near Alexandria.
34-acre site acquired for $165M in 2022; first phase shell construction ~$5M.
Local opposition pushing for affordable housing swap; county rejected proposal.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group is advancing Plaza 500 South, a 226,000-SF two-story data center in Fairfax County, Va., on a 34-acre site purchased for $165M in 2022. The first construction phase shell work is budgeted at $5M. Community opposition has surfaced over electrical infrastructure and housing needs, with residents proposing the county swap the site for affordable housing development elsewhere.
stories.impact: Data center growth in Northern Virginia corridors signals sustained demand for power-dense infrastructure and tech-adjacent real estate, but it consumes large land parcels at premium prices that might otherwise support multifamily. In Fairfax/Alexandria submarket, this may push residential developers toward infill or redevelopment rather than greenfield acquisition, and could intensify zoning/entitlement friction—as seen here—around mixed-use vs. single-use allocation.

stories.title: United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building
stories.excerpt: United Talent Agency (UTA) and Empire State Realty Trust, Inc. announced that the agency will move its New York offices to the Empire State Building next summer. The new office will feature 100,948 square feet across four floors and will house the agency’s filmed entertainment, creators, news, publishing, theatre, music, speakers, sports, brand advisory and executive search businesses. “We are pleased to welcome United Talent Agency to the ‘World’s Most Famous Building’ and to support the firm’s continued growth,” said Ryan Kass, EVP, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT. “The Empire State Building fosters collaboration, attracts top talent, enhances the employee experience, and is a premier destination for the world’s most innovative and influential businesses.” Mitchell Konsker, Benjamin Bass and Harrison Potter of JLL represented UTA in the lease negotiations. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle of ESRT and Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Neil Rubin, Brent Ozarowski, Zachary Weil and Cole Gendels of Newmark represented the property owner. The post United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: United Talent Agency leasing 100,948 SF across 4 floors at Empire State Building, NYC.
Move from existing NYC office; consolidates multiple divisions under one roof.
Signals continued demand for iconic, branded office space by media/entertainment firms in Manhattan.
stories.summary: United Talent Agency (UTA) is relocating to 100,948 SF across four floors of the Empire State Building in Manhattan, consolidating its filmed entertainment, creators, news, publishing, and other divisions. The deal brings visibility to ESRT's leasing strength and reinforces the building's appeal to premium tenants seeking marquee addresses and collaboration-focused environments.
stories.impact: UTA's commitment to large, contiguous office space in a prestige Manhattan location—and near museums/cultural anchors—suggests sustained demand for office by high-margin professional services and media firms that attract talent. However, it also signals consolidation (one firm taking one large block) rather than broad office absorption, limiting overall market tightness. Residential developers should note that trophy office lease-ups do not translate to adjacent multifamily demand; entertainment-sector job growth is modest relative to tech or finance. Limited relevance to multifamily unless vacancy elsewhere in Midtown East creates conversion-to-residential opportunities.

stories.title: Real Estate Firm Yuco, AI Company Elite Each Take 7K SF at 757 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: Two tenants have signed on for 6,960 square feet each at Midtown East’s 757 Third Avenue, Commercial Observer has learned. Yuco Management, a New York City-based real estate development and management firm, inked a deal to take over the entire 27th floor of the 26-story Class A office building owned by New York Life Real Estate Investors, according to landlord broker JLL. Yuco will relocate to 757 Third Avenue from its current offices at 200 Park Avenue. Meanwhile, Elite, an artificial intelligence-powered legal software firm, signed a lease for the entire 22nd floor of the property. The deal seems to represent Elite’s first New York City office, as it has its headquarters in El Segundo, Calif. Cushman & Wakefield’s Mark Weiss and Jenna Catalon represented Yuco in its deal, while Adam Ardise, Stephen Bellwood and Rachel Rosenfeld, also from C&W, brokered the deal for Elite. JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Clark Finney, Simon Landmann, Kristen Morgan and Lance Yasinsky represented the landlord in each lease. “Every building has a different story to tell in today’s market,” JLL’s Konsker said in a statement. “Our role is to understand where tenant demand is headed and help ownership align its asset with those evolving expectations. The recent activity at 757 Third Avenue reflects the strength of Midtown leasing fundamentals, the quality of its ownership and the value tenants place on well-executed repositioning strategies.” The lengths of the leases and the exact asking rents were unclear, but JLL said rents in the office tower are in the high $70s per square foot. Spokespeople for C&W, Yuco Management and Elite did not immediately respond to requests for comment. News of the leasing at 757 Third Avenue comes as New York Life invests in repositioning and reintroducing the property to the market after acquiring it from BGO through a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure sale in October 2025. New York Life “continues to enhance the property with planned cosmetic renovations t
bullets: Yuco Management (RE dev firm) and Elite (AI legal software) each lease 7K SF at 757 Third Ave, Midtown East.
Yuco relocates from 200 Park Ave; Elite takes first NYC office (HQ in El Segundo, Calif.).
Two floor leases in Class A Midtown East office signal selective, smaller-footprint tenant demand.
stories.summary: Yuco Management and Elite each signed for approximately 7,000 SF (full floors 27 and 22, respectively) at 757 Third Avenue in Midtown East, Manhattan. Yuco is relocating from 200 Park Avenue, while Elite is establishing its first New York office, having been headquartered in El Segundo, California.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily demand. Both leases are office-centric and represent small, specific tenant moves rather than broad employment growth or corporate relocation to NYC. Elite's expansion to NYC is noted but does not signal a large-scale hiring wave. The leasing activity in Midtown East reflects selective tenant interest in refurbished Class A stock, but does not indicate demand drivers (wage growth, job density, population influx) that would materially increase residential absorption in nearby residential submarkets like Kips Bay or Murray Hill.

stories.title: Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo
stories.excerpt: Berkadia arranged the sale and acquisition financing of two value-add, neighboring Castro Valley multifamily communities with a total of 135 units. They included the Cedars, an 83-unit property that sold for $21.45 million, and Creekside Terrace, a 52-unit property that sold for $14.1 million. Senior managing director Jason Parr, managing director Scott MacDonald and director John Hansen of Berkadia San Francisco arranged the sale on behalf of Felson Companies, the original developer and longtime owner of both communities. Managing director Clay Akiwenzie and director Hank Workman of Berkadia San Francisco arranged a seven-year fixed-rate Freddie Mac loan with full-term interest-only on behalf of San Francisco-based Prime Residential. “Properties held by a single owner for over 50 years are incredibly rare in the Bay Area, and the value-add potential here drew significant investor demand,” said MacDonald. “Our investment sales team partnered closely with our mortgage banking colleagues to navigate a complex environment and deliver an exceptional outcome for our client.” The post Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Castro Valley, Calif.: 135-unit multifamily duo (Cedars 83-unit, Creekside Terrace 52-unit) sold for $35.55M combined.
Original owner (Felson Companies) held both 50+ years; Prime Residential acquired with Freddie Mac financing.
7-year fixed-rate, full-term interest-only loan reflects value-add stabilization strategy.
stories.summary: Two adjacent Castro Valley multifamily properties totaling 135 units—the Cedars (83 units, $21.45M) and Creekside Terrace (52 units, $14.1M)—transacted after 50+ years under original ownership by Felson Companies. Buyer Prime Residential secured a Freddie Mac 7-year fixed-rate loan with interest-only structure, arranged by Berkadia.
stories.impact: Bay Area multifamily turnover from long-hold original owners signals increasing investor appetite for stabilized, value-add assets in supply-constrained suburban markets. Blended price of $263K per unit reflects modest pricing relative to Bay Area urban cores, indicating Castro Valley (Oakland suburb) remains affordable relative to coastal markets—a draw for acquisition-focused operators. The interest-only financing structure suggests lender and buyer expect modest upside from operational/rent growth, not repositioning; this reflects tighter Bay Area underwriting and lower return expectations in a mature, saturated market.

stories.title: Luxury Menswear Brand ISAIA Takes 13K SF at Feil’s 257 Park Avenue South
stories.excerpt: ISAIA, a luxury menswear brand out of Naples, Italy, has signed a 13,000-square-foot lease renewal on the 10th floor of the Feil Organization’s 257 Park Avenue South, the property owner announced Tuesday. Feil said the brand has “recommitted” to its corporate showroom space at the property, also known as the Gramercy Park Building. The length of the lease is for 10 years. Feil did not disclose the asking rent for the space. The average asking rent for office space in the Gramercy area was $89.09 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers data. “Continuing our relationship with ISAIA speaks to both the quality of the space and the Feil Organization’s ability to deliver unique environments that meet the needs of a diverse roster of tenants,” Feil’s Andrew Wiener, who represented the landlord in-house alongside Robert Fisher, said in a statement. “Their long-term recommitment is another testament to the strength of the property, which is currently 97 percent leased.” ISAIA was represented by Alexander Chudnoff and Henry Korzec from JLL. Chudnoff and Korzec did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “Our showroom at 257 Park Avenue South is an extension of the ISAIA brand and plays an important role in how we present our collections and connect with clients,” James Shay, president of ISAIA, said in a statement. “The unique character of the building, its prime location and the support of the Feil Organization made renewing our lease the right decision.” Situated at the corner of East 21st Street, 257 Park Avenue South is a 20-story office property with tenants including financial services firm Argentic Investment Management and fintech company AngelList. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: ISAIA (luxury menswear, Naples, Italy) renewed 13K SF on floor 10 of 257 Park Ave South, Manhattan.
10-year lease renewal; Gramercy Park/Flatiron submarket at $89.09 psf (Q2 2026 average).
Corporate showroom renewal indicates ancillary office/showroom demand in secondary Manhattan submarkets.
stories.summary: ISAIA, a luxury menswear brand from Naples, Italy, has renewed its 13,000-SF corporate showroom on the 10th floor of 257 Park Avenue South (Gramercy Park Building) for a 10-year term. No rent disclosed, but market average for Gramercy area office was $89.09 psf in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Minimal impact on multifamily. Luxury brand renewals in secondary Manhattan office markets like Gramercy Park reflect sustained demand for branded, prestige office/showroom space but do not signal residential demand drivers. Showroom/corporate office users do not materially increase foot traffic or ancillary demand (dining, retail, services) that would drive adjacent residential upside. Gramercy Park itself is a mature, affluent residential neighborhood; any multifamily development would be limited by zoning and local opposition, not office market dynamics.

stories.title: Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion
stories.excerpt: Resorts World New York City on Tuesday opened the first floor of its commercial casino, adding 1,400 slot machines less than three months after the new facility became the city’s first full-fledged casino. The move, which brings the number of slot machines onsite to 3,900, is expected to significantly increase gross gaming revenue and commensurate taxes to New York State, according to Resorts World. “Since the moment we opened, our slot machines have been incredibly popular with the public, generating more than $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks,” said Robert DeSalvio, president of Genting Americas East. “With these 1,400 new games, we will be able to accommodate more patrons and generate even more in taxes for the state.” The gaming operation represents the first stage of a planned $5.5-billion buildout at the site. By 2030, RWNYC intends to operate a 350,000-square-foot gaming floor with 6,000 slot machines and 800 live table games, a 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 2,000 hotel rooms and more than 10 acres of public green space. The post Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Resorts World NYC opened floor 1 of casino with 1,400 new slot machines; 3,900 total onsite.
$230M gross gaming revenue in 11 weeks; planned 2030 expansion to 6K slots, 800 tables, 2K hotel rooms.
5.5B buildout includes 7K-seat entertainment venue and 10+ acres of public green space.
stories.summary: Resorts World New York City (Queens) added 1,400 slot machines (floor one of casino), bringing total to 3,900, less than three months after opening. The property generated $230M in gross gaming revenue in 11 weeks. Planned $5.5B expansion by 2030 includes 6,000 slots, 800 tables, 2,000 hotel rooms, a 7,000-seat entertainment venue, and 10+ acres of public green space.
stories.impact: Resorts World represents major employment, entertainment, and visitor draw to Queens, driving both daytime foot traffic and potential inbound migration for gaming/hospitality jobs. However, onsite 2,000 hotel rooms will cannibalize demand for nearby hotel and short-term rental product. Longer-term, sustained employment and entertainment draw (7K-seat venue, restaurants, retail) may support adjacent residential demand in Queens neighborhoods, but Resorts World's self-contained hotel supply limits multifamily rent growth in immediate submarket. Broader impact: job growth and venue amenity in Queens could attract younger, entertainment-focused residents to outer-borough submarkets, moderating demand pressure in Manhattan and increasing relative affordability of Queens residential.

stories.title: Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood
stories.excerpt: Calare Properties has acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-square-foot industrial warehouse/flex condominium in Boston’s Hyde Park section. Terms were not disclosed. The urban property sits within a 22-acre, 10-building industrial and mixed-use site with a manufacturing history dating back to the early 1900s. 128 CRE brokered the sale. The building is nearly 50% leased to a long-term tenant. The available 24,000-square-foot space provides tenants with an opportunity to secure a long-term lease in a market where quality industrial space is scarce, according to Calare. The new ownership is executing a capital improvement plan. “Quality industrial buildings of this scale are exceptionally rare in Boston, and that scarcity is exactly what drives our strategy,” said Tim Collis, SVP at Calare. “Being part of an established, well-run campus gives us both immediate stability and long-term upside as we execute our planned capital program and long-term leasing strategy.” The post Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Bldg K, 42K-SF industrial flex in Boston's Hyde Park.
Nearly 50% pre-leased; 24K SF available; property part of 22-acre, 10-building industrial campus.
Scarcity-driven strategy: quality industrial supply constrained in Boston; capital plan underway.
stories.summary: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-SF industrial/flex warehouse condominium in Hyde Park, Boston, as part of a larger 22-acre industrial campus. The property is nearly 50% leased, with 24,000 SF available. Calare cited scarcity of quality industrial space in Boston as the primary driver and is executing a capital improvement plan.
stories.impact: Industrial scarcity in Boston's urban/inner-ring markets (Hyde Park) signals sustained logistics/light manufacturing demand and limits multifamily land conversion in these areas. However, Calare's acquisition of a mixed-use industrial campus (manufacturing heritage, 10-building spread) suggests potential for long-term repositioning toward mixed-use or residential if industrial tenants vacate or rents soften. Developers should monitor Hyde Park and similar Boston neighborhood submarkets for future residential redevelopment windows, but near-term industrial fundamentals are tight, keeping land values high and limiting multifamily groundbreaking.

stories.title: Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale
stories.excerpt: The Mogharebi Group (TMG) represented JBT Property Management in the sale of Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit multifamily community located at 475-585 W. Sierra Ave. in Fresno, to a private buyer for $44 million. TMG’s team of EVP Otto Ozen, SVP Brian Nakamura and Svp Nazli Santana represented the seller. “As a first-time-on-the-market property since its construction in 1986, the asset offered a rare opportunity to acquire a well-maintained, large-scale property directly from original ownership,” said Nakamura. “With its substantial unit count in a supply‑constrained market, the property immediately enhances the buyer’s regional presence—supporting stronger operational alignment, improved management efficiency, and meaningful performance gains, all without requiring significant capital investment.” Maroa Park Apartments benefits from immediate income upside through operational efficiencies while benefiting from strong in-place market fundamentals, relative affordability, and a strategic Central Valley location that continues to support long-term population and employment growth, according to TMG The post Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Fresno, Calif.: Maroa Park Apartments (248 units, built 1986) sold for $44M from original ownership.
First-time market sale; no major capital investment required; supply-constrained Central Valley market.
Blended price ~$177K per unit; strong in-place fundamentals cited by broker.
stories.summary: Fresno, Calif.: Maroa Park Apartments (248 units, built 1986) sold for $44M from original ownership. First-time market sale; no major capital investment required; supply-constrained Central Valley market. Blended price ~$177K per unit; strong in-place fundamentals cited.
stories.impact: Central Valley multifamily sales from long-term holders signal tightening supply and investor recognition of Fresno's demographic tailwinds (population in-migration from coastal California, job growth in logistics/agriculture/food processing). Blended $177K per-unit valuation reflects Fresno's relative affordability vs. Bay Area and LA; this attracts acquisition-focused operators seeking stabilized, non-performing assets without major repositioning risk. For developers, this highlights Central Valley (Fresno, Bakersfield, Visalia) as acquisition and potential new-construction target markets where land is cheaper, construction costs are lower, and rent growth is supported by supply scarcity and in-migration. Window to develop new product in Fresno likely remains open before valuations rise further.

stories.title: Oceanwide Plaza Sale Set to Advance After L.A. Drops Objections
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles’ Oceanwide Plaza, a massive downtown development stuck in limbo so long it became tagged and transformed into the infamous Graffiti Towers, has been cleared for sale. The long process of selecting a new developer after the original Chinese developer went bankrupt may finally be coming to a close. KPC Group, a Southern California real estate firm headed by Dr. Kali Chaudhuri, and Lendlease Americas will contribute $517 million in cash and credit to acquire the unfinished project, and agreed to spend roughly $800 million to finish the trio of towers, located downtown near Crypto.com Arena. “I will be delighted to enter an order confirming the plan,” said Deborah Saltzman, a federal bankruptcy judge, during an hours-long hearing in a Downtown Los Angeles courtroom on Monday. After a break for negotiations, the parties returned to the courtroom for a brief afternoon session confirming the details of the arrangement. The plan approval comes despite the city attorney’s objections to the KPC/Lendlease bid. In a May filing, the city, in its role as a creditor and governmental entity overseeing regulations and entitlements for the project, noted after multiple meetings, site visits and analysis that the bidder didn’t offer a complete plan that showed they had the funding and resources to fully finish. The City Attorney’s Office dropped its objections to the modified bankruptcy plan Monday after securing stronger commitments from the proposed buyer. The final version of the court’s orders will be filed Tuesday. KPC has a six-month window to complete the sale. Otherwise a backup buyer can be selected. In recent months, a competing bid by Sean Burton‘s CityView was seen as a potential backup. KPC is also required, within 90 days, to remove the graffiti that has adorned the tower since 2024, when taggers began climbing and bombing the towers. Long considered an eyesore, a potential embarrassment during the upcoming 2028 Olympics, and a symbol of the city’s stru
bullets: Oceanwide Plaza (Los Angeles downtown, $1B luxury project stalled since 2019) advancing after city dropped objections.
KPC Group and Lendlease contributing $517M (cash/credit) to acquire; ~$800M additional to complete trio of towers.
Complex bankruptcy process moving forward; timeline to completion not disclosed.
stories.summary: Los Angeles' Oceanwide Plaza, a three-tower luxury development stalled since 2019 when original Chinese developer ran out of funding, is advancing after the City of Los Angeles dropped objections. KPC Group and Lendlease will contribute $517M to acquire the project and commit ~$800M more to complete it. The development is located downtown near Crypto.com Arena.
stories.impact: Oceanwide Plaza resolution is critical for downtown LA residential supply and investor confidence but represents mixed impact. Completion of 3 luxury towers will add supply but is heavily skewed toward high-end units, not addressing LA's acute affordable housing shortage. The long bankruptcy delay (2019–2026+) signals execution risk and underwriter caution on complex urban projects; future LA downtown residential development may face higher capital requirements and longer entitlement cycles. For multifamily developers, the Oceanwide turnaround underscores the importance of strong, diversified capital and experienced management in urban repositioning—and the risk of stalled, value-destructive projects in downtown markets with regulatory friction. The deal's success may prompt renewed investment in downtown LA infill, but only by well-capitalized operators.

stories.title: AI Company Mercor Takes 26K SF at Durst’s One World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: One World Trade Center is almost 100 percent leased thanks to the latest deal with an artificial intelligence company, Commercial Observer has learned. Mercor, which employs human experts to train AI algorithms, has inked a five-year, 25,550-square-foot deal on the 77th floor of the Lower Manhattan office tower, the Durst Organization announced Tuesday. Durst owns the 104-story building along with the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. The exact asking rent was not disclosed, but a source close to the deal told CO that the asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. Durst was represented in-house by Eric Engelhardt, Karen Rose and Sayo Kamara, along with David Falk, Peter Shimkin, Hal Stein, Nathan Kropp and Paige Raisides from Newmark. Harry Singer from CBRE represented Mercor. The brokers did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “One World Trade Center was built for forward-looking, cutting-edge companies looking for a world-class home in New York City,” Jody Durst, president of Durst, said in a statement. “We look forward to welcoming Mercor to the building.” Mercor’s website says its headquarters is located in San Francisco, and does not list a New York City address. However, company CEO Brendan Foody previously posted on LinkedIn that the company would be expanding to New York City in January. It is unclear whether Foody was referencing the deal at One World Trade Center. “New York puts us close to some of the most important enterprises in the world, and to some of the highest caliber talent,” Koby Twist, special initiatives lead at Mercor, said in a statement. “One World Trade Center gives our team a home at the center of both.” One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, was built by the Port Authority and Durst in a public-private partnership. Other recent tenants include fashion and culture publication W Magazine, global financial services company Ameriprise Financial, and energy- and infrastruct
bullets: Mercor (AI algorithm training firm) leased 25,550 SF on floor 77 of One World Trade Center, Lower Manhattan.
5-year lease; asking rent high $80s–$90s psf; One WTC now ~100% leased.
Tech/AI sector continues NYC office demand in prestige locations; Durst represented building.
stories.summary: Mercor, an AI company that trains algorithms using human experts, signed a five-year lease for 25,550 SF on the 77th floor of One World Trade Center in Lower Manhattan. Asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. The lease brings One World Trade Center to near 100% occupancy.
stories.impact: AI sector demand for premium Manhattan office—particularly in iconic, Class-A towers—reflects sustained, high-margin employment in NYC and reinforces Lower Manhattan's recovery as a tech/innovation hub post-9/11. However, Mercor's lease is a single, large block by a specialized, high-skill firm; it does not signal broad office sector demand or wage/employment growth sufficient to materially drive multifamily demand in Lower Manhattan or nearby Financial District neighborhoods. Tech office tenants tend to cluster, creating subcritical residential mass; broader driver of residential demand in Lower Manhattan remains tourism, hospitality, and mixed-use retail. Limited relevance to multifamily development unless Mercor lease is part of larger Lower Manhattan tech cluster formation, which is not yet evident.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami (Ponce-Davis): $18.9M mansion sale (10K SF, 7 bed/7.5 bath, built 2026) to telecom exec.
Wynwood, Miami: $24.5M commercial sale (13K SF, 2-building, mixed-use) to Moishe Mana.
South Florida luxury residential and commercial activity reflects capital inflow and investor confidence.
stories.summary: South Florida recorded major transactions: a newly built ($19M) mansion in Miami's Ponce-Davis neighborhood (7 bed/7.5 bath) sold to a telecommunications executive, and a $24.5M Wynwood commercial property (13K SF, two buildings with Pastis/Apparatus tenants) sold to an investor linked to developer Moishe Mana.
stories.impact: Miami luxury residential and Wynwood mixed-use commercial activity signals sustained capital inflow, trophy asset demand, and developer confidence in South Florida. However, these are single high-ticket transactions, not indicators of broad multifamily demand or supply tightness. Luxury acquisitions by executives and developers suggest wealth concentration and speculative investment rather than primary household formation; does not necessarily translate to multifamily rent growth or new development demand. Wynwood's retail/mixed-use strength (Pastis, Apparatus) indicates neighborhood vibrancy but does not drive multifamily if zoning/land use remain retail/commercial. For multifamily developers, South Florida's luxury market shows capital appetite but also hints at potential oversupply of high-end product and lower-income household squeeze; opportunity lies in affordable/workforce housing, not luxury.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing & Capstone Equities developing 316 affordable units (100%) at 21155 Califa St, Woodland Hills.
7-story new construction replacing 35K-SF commercial; part of Warner Center affordability boom amid Kroenke investment.
Project tied to largest new construction permit (first week July 2026); expedited approval under LA Exec Directive 1.
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street in Woodland Hills to develop 316 income-restricted units across seven stories, demolishing a 35,000-SF commercial building. The Green at Warner Center project qualifies for expedited approval under LA's Executive Directive 1 (100% affordable). Development activity is intensifying in Warner Center as LA Rams owner Stan Kroenke advances a 52-acre mixed-use master plan nearby.
stories.impact: Warner Center's transformation into an affordability corridor—driven by Kroenke's $billions mixed-use investment and city incentives—signals massive multifamily development potential. Elysian and Capstone's focus on 100% affordable (likely low-income) product indicates policy-driven development (tax credits, city subsidies, expedited entitlements) rather than market-rate demand. For developers, this highlights Los Angeles' shift toward city-subsidized, dense affordable multifamily on underutilized commercial/office sites. Key second-order effect: Kroenke's mixed-use anchor (office, retail, entertainment) in Woodland Hills will drive ancillary jobs and foot traffic, making Warner Center a magnet for both affordable and market-rate multifamily. Developers should pursue mixed-income (LIHTC + market-rate crossover) projects near Kroenke's development to capture both subsidy and market demand.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, Texas (Austin suburb): Selected Griffin Swinerton to lead downtown revamp; target 2028 launch.
City tripled in size since 2020; targeting walkable, mixed-use downtown with parking and industry-specific districts.
Griffin Swinerton will function as city staff; managing infrastructure, financing, community input.
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, one of the nation's fastest-growing Austin suburbs, selected Griffin Swinerton (JV of Griffin Structures and Swinerton) to lead a downtown transformation targeting 2028. The developer will operate as quasi-city staff, evaluating infrastructure, financing, and community needs to create a walkable downtown with parking improvements and industry-specific districts. Liberty Hill tripled in size since the 2020 census.
stories.impact: Liberty Hill's explosive growth (3x since 2020) and strategic downtown investment signal acute demand for suburban multifamily and mixed-use product near Austin. Griffin Swinerton's role as master planner/infrastructure developer indicates the city's commitment to coordinated growth and removes typical entitlement friction. For multifamily developers, Liberty Hill represents a high-velocity, pro-growth market with streamlined approval and built-in demand from in-migration. Downtown anchor (walkability, amenities, parking) will support Class-A multifamily at scale; early acquisition of nearby residential sites before 2028 launch will be advantageous. Broader Austin metro trend: outer suburbs (Liberty Hill, Pflugerville, Williamson County) are becoming primary multifamily growth markets as inner-Austin/central neighborhoods saturate and affordability declines.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago (South Loop): CMK Companies' 910 South Wells (28-story, 350 units) secures piles permit; foundation phase underway.
Mixed-use (3.3K SF retail, 350 residential units, parking, riverwalk); part of $2B Riverline master plan.
CMK secured $83M construction loan (Wintrust) for 388-unit Phase 1 at 1010 South Wells; concurrent construction.
stories.summary: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells, a 28-story mixed-use tower in Chicago's South Loop (350 residential units, 3,300 SF retail, parking, riverwalk access), has obtained its piles permit and is entering foundation construction. The project is part of CMK's $2B Riverline master plan on eight acres. CMK simultaneously is building 1010 South Wells (388 units) with an $83M construction loan from Wintrust Bank, using general contractor Brandt's Build at both sites.
stories.impact: CMK's concurrent delivery of two major residential towers (738 units combined, $2B+ capital) in South Loop demonstrates developer confidence in Chicago's core market and signals sustained multifamily demand in urban riverfront/transit-adjacent submarkets. Riverline's scale, financing, and master-plan approach attract institutional capital and set a high bar for new South Loop entrants. For multifamily developers, South Loop's riverfront activation (Riverline, riverwalk connectivity, public amenities) indicates a maturing, walkable urban neighborhood with strong renter demand. Secondary impact: CMK's success may inflate land values and entitlement timelines for competitors, making speculative land acquisition in South Loop riskier. Developers should focus on alternative South Loop micromarkets or transit-adjacent corridors (Red Line, etc.) where land is cheaper and approval less competitive.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas (Austrian wellness company) planning $800M resort on Trinity River; seeking 2027 groundbreaking.
24 acres, 500K SF; adding skybridge and non-residential zones to 2027 plan; 2026 still pending city approvals.
Company credibility questioned: NYT revealed misrepresentation of international portfolio; only one spa built (Bucharest).
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness resort operator, is seeking city approval for design modifications (skybridge, non-residential zones) to its $800M, 24-acre Trinity River resort development (500K SF, thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, retractable glass roof) targeting 2027 groundbreaking. However, a New York Times investigation revealed Therme misrepresented its global portfolio to Ontario authorities, claiming six facilities under construction when only one spa (Bucharest) was completed.
stories.impact: Therme Dallas's credibility crisis and history of misrepresentation raise significant execution and financing risk. The $800M capital requirement and 500K-SF footprint make this a major mixed-use project with potential to anchor Trinity River waterfront. However, developer credibility questions may deter institutional capital and extend city approval timelines. For multifamily developers, Therme's project uncertainty presents both risk and opportunity: if Therme stalls or scales back, the Trinity River site may become available for alternative development; if Therme succeeds, the resort will anchor a major destination/amenity that supports adjacent multifamily demand. Given developer concerns, nearby multifamily projects should assume longer approval and financing cycles, and should consider contingency sites in case Therme falters.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secures $28M mid-construction financing (S3 Capital) for The Albritton, Friendswood, Texas mixed-use.
111 multifamily units, 11K SF retail, 9K SF office; 70% LTV; completion 2028.
Tannos also building $750M Friendswood City Center (2024 start) and $1B Magnolia Town Center with Maritia.
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28M in mid-construction financing (70% LTV) from S3 Capital for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas (111 units, 11K SF retail, 9K SF office). Completion targeted for 2028. Tannos is simultaneously developing Friendswood City Center ($750M, started spring 2024) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B with Maritia in partnership).
stories.impact: Tannos's three-project portfolio (The Albritton, Friendswood City Center, Magnolia Town Center totaling ~$2B+ capital) demonstrates robust developer activity and lender appetite in suburban Houston/Galveston corridor. Friendswood (25 miles SE of Houston, 42K residents) and Magnolia represent exurban, car-dependent growth markets with sustained population in-migration and affordability relative to Houston core. Mid-construction financing at 70% LTV on The Albritton suggests lender confidence in execution and market absorption. For multifamily developers, Houston-area suburbs offer lower land costs, faster entitlement, and strong demographic tailwinds (population growth, job expansion in logistics/petrochemicals) vs. central Houston. Tannos's success signals that mixed-use (multifamily + retail + office) is viable in suburban markets if master-planned correctly; however, 2028 completion on The Albritton suggests longer development/absorption cycles in outer suburbs vs. primary markets.
